specification.topic-statement: The scrollback buffer stores an unbounded sequence of logical text lines in a single contiguous byte array and maintains pre-computed display layout data for each line to support efficient rendering and terminal-width reflow.

scope.in-scope: Text storage layout, line indexing, dynamic growth strategy, append operation, layout computation on append, O(n)-arithmetic reflow on terminal-width change, physical-row lookup by row number, and all utility operations on stored lines.
scope.boundary:
The per-line layout calculation — segment widths, physical row counts, display widths — is performed by the scrollback layout subsystem. This specification covers when layout is triggered and what the result is stored as, not the internal calculation.
The layout subsystem computes a segment-width array for a line by splitting the text at newline characters and measuring the display width of each segment in terminal columns. It uses this to compute the number of physical rows the line occupies at a given terminal width.
How stored lines are rendered to the terminal output buffer is handled by the layer rendering pipeline and is out of scope here.

field.name: text buffer
field.note: all lines stored end-to-end; each line's bytes followed by a null terminator; never shrinks

field.name: text offsets
field.note: byte offset in text buffer where each line's text begins; one entry per logical line

field.name: text lengths
field.note: byte length of each line's text, not including the null terminator; one entry per logical line

field.name: layouts
field.note: pre-computed layout for each line; one entry per logical line

field.name: line count
field.note: number of logical lines currently stored

field.name: line capacity
field.note: allocated slots in the offset, length, and layout arrays; doubles when count would exceed it

field.name: buffer used
field.note: bytes currently occupied in the text buffer, including null terminators

field.name: buffer capacity
field.note: total allocated bytes in the text buffer; doubles when an append would exceed it

field.name: cached width
field.note: terminal width in columns used for the most recent layout calculation; -1 or 0 before first calculation

field.name: total physical lines
field.note: sum of physical_lines across all stored logical lines; updated on append and reflow

field.name: display width
field.note: total display width of the line in terminal columns, excluding ANSI escape sequences and newline characters

field.name: physical lines
field.note: number of terminal rows the line occupies at the cached terminal width

field.name: newline count
field.note: number of newline characters embedded in the line text

field.name: segment widths
field.note: display width of each segment between (or after) newlines; length is newline_count + 1

behavior.initialization: A new scrollback buffer is created with an initial line capacity of 16 and an initial text buffer capacity of 1024 bytes. All counts start at zero. The cached width is set to indicate that no layout has been calculated.
behavior.append:
A logical line is appended by supplying its text bytes and length. The following steps execute in order:
If the current line count equals the line capacity, the capacity is doubled and the three parallel arrays (text offsets, text lengths, layouts) are each reallocated to the new capacity. If any reallocation fails, an out-of-memory error is returned and no state is modified.
If the current buffer usage plus the incoming length plus one (for the null terminator) would exceed the buffer capacity, the capacity is doubled repeatedly until it is sufficient. If the reallocation fails, an out-of-memory error is returned.
The text bytes are copied into the text buffer at the current write position (buffer used). A null terminator byte is written immediately after. The text offset for the new line is set to the write position before the copy. The text length for the new line is set to the supplied length. Buffer used is advanced by length plus one (for the null terminator).
The layout subsystem is invoked to compute the layout record for the new line at the current cached terminal width. The computed layout is stored in the layouts array at the new line's index. The total physical lines counter is increased by the new line's physical lines count.
The line count is incremented. The operation returns success.
Each line's text in the buffer is null-terminated for safe use as a C string, but the stored text length excludes the terminator. The pointer returned by the line-text accessor points directly into the buffer and is valid until the next modification.
behavior.layout-reflow:
When the terminal width changes, physical row counts must be recalculated. The reflow operation iterates over all stored lines and, for each line, recomputes its physical row count using only the existing segment-width array and the new terminal width: each segment's physical rows is the ceiling of its display width divided by the terminal width, with a minimum of one row per segment. No UTF-8 scanning or newline counting is performed. The total physical lines counter is recomputed from the new per-line counts. The cached width is updated.
If the cached width already equals the requested width, the operation is a no-op.
Because segment widths are pre-computed at append time, a resize requires only arithmetic over the stored integers. The text buffer is not scanned during reflow.
behavior.physical-row-lookup:
Given a physical row number (zero-indexed across the entire scrollback), the corresponding logical line index and the row offset within that line are found by scanning the layout array from the first line, accumulating physical row counts, until the target row falls within a line's range. The output is the logical line index and the row offset (how many rows into that line the target row falls).
Lookup is O(n) in the number of logical lines. There is no index structure for random access by physical row.
behavior.clear: Clearing the buffer resets the line count, buffer-used, and total physical lines counter to zero. The segment-width arrays owned by each layout record are freed. The text buffer capacity and line array capacity are preserved; no memory is released. The buffer is ready to accept new lines immediately.
behavior.copy: Copying from a source scrollback to a destination scrollback appends each source line to the destination in order by calling the append operation. The destination's cached width determines the layout of the copied lines, which may differ from the source's layout if the two have different cached widths.
behavior.line-text-retrieval: A line's text is retrieved by supplying its zero-indexed line number. The operation returns a pointer directly into the text buffer and the line's byte length. The pointer is valid until the next append, clear, or copy operation on the same buffer. The caller must not free the pointer.
behavior.display-column-to-byte-offset: Given a logical line index and a target display column, the byte offset within that line's text at which the target column begins is computed by walking the UTF-8 text, tracking the display column while skipping ANSI escape sequences. The walk stops when the column count reaches the target. If the target column exceeds the line's total display width, the byte offset of the end of the text is returned.
behavior.no-capacity-limit: There is no configured maximum for the number of lines or total bytes. The buffer grows without bound as lines are appended, limited only by available memory. An allocation failure during growth returns an error to the caller; the buffer remains in its pre-growth state.
